walkthrough.title: Meet the Components
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Lost Cities set up. First, find the deck of sixty cards. There are five colors: yellow, blue, white, green, and red. Each color has number cards from two through ten and three investment cards marked with a handshake symbol.

walkthrough.title: Place the Board
walkthrough.text: Take the game board and place it between the two players. The board has five columns, one for each color, and these are the discard piles. If you do not have the board, just leave space between you for five discard areas, one per color. Either way works perfectly fine.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Deck
walkthrough.text: Gather all sixty cards together and give them a thorough shuffle. Mix them up well since this is the only deck in the game. A good riffle shuffle or two should do the trick.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal eight cards face down to each player. Pick up your hand and take a look, but keep your cards hidden from your opponent. Your hand is private information throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Create the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: Place the remaining cards face down in a stack next to the board. This is the draw pile and it is the game's timer. When the last card is drawn, the round ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Expedition Area
walkthrough.text: Each player should have an open space in front of them for their expeditions. You will play cards here in columns by color during the game. Right now this area should be completely empty. Grab a piece of paper and pencil for scoring and you are ready to go.

summary.title: Meet the Components
bullets: 60 cards total — 5 colors (yellow, blue, white, green, red)
Each color: numbers 2-10 (9 cards) + 3 investment/wager cards

summary.title: Place the Board
bullets: Place board between players — marks 5 color discard piles
No board? Leave space for 5 discard areas by color

summary.title: Shuffle the Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 60 cards together into one deck

summary.title: Deal Starting Hands
bullets: Deal 8 cards face-down to each player
Hands are private — keep hidden from opponent

summary.title: Create the Draw Pile
bullets: Remaining cards = face-down draw pile next to board
Draw pile is the game timer — round ends when it's empty

summary.title: Set Up Your Expedition Area
bullets: Open space in front of each player for expedition columns
Starts empty — you'll play cards here during the game
Grab paper and pencil for scoring

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Lost Cities is a two-player card game about mounting expeditions to distant lands. You will play numbered cards in ascending order to build up expedition columns in front of you. Each expedition you start costs twenty points, so you need to play enough high cards to make it worthwhile.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Two Steps
walkthrough.text: First, you must either play a card or discard a card. Second, you must draw a card. That is it. Play or discard, then draw. Let's break down each step.

walkthrough.title: Step One: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: To play a card, take it from your hand and place it face up on your expedition column of that color. Here is the critical rule: cards in each expedition must be played in ascending order. You can skip numbers, but you can never go backwards. If your red expedition already has a five, you can play a seven or a ten, but never a three or a four.

walkthrough.title: Step One: Discard a Card
walkthrough.text: Instead of playing a card to your expedition, you can discard a card face up onto its matching color's discard pile on the board. Be careful though. Your opponent can pick up that card on their next turn. Discarding is how you get rid of cards you cannot use, but it can also feed your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Step Two: Draw a Card
walkthrough.text: After playing or discarding, you must draw one card. You can draw from the face-down draw pile, or you can pick up the top card from any color's discard pile. There is one restriction: you cannot discard a card and then draw from that same color's discard pile on the same turn.

walkthrough.title: Investment Cards
walkthrough.text: Each color has three investment cards, also called wager cards. These are the cards with the handshake symbol instead of a number. Investment cards multiply your expedition's final score. One investment card doubles the score, two triple it, and all three quadruple it. The catch is that investment cards must be played before any numbered cards in that color. They multiply both profits and losses.

walkthrough.title: The Twenty-Point Cost
walkthrough.text: Here is the key tension in Lost Cities. Every expedition you start begins at negative twenty points. That means the numbered cards you play need to add up to more than twenty just to break even. If you start a red expedition and only play a three and a five, that is eight minus twenty, which equals negative twelve points. Only start expeditions where you expect to play enough cards to turn a profit.

walkthrough.title: The Eight-Card Bonus
walkthrough.text: This bonus is added after the multiplier is applied, so it is always a flat twenty. Getting to eight cards is hard but extremely rewarding, especially when combined with investment cards.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: The round ends immediately when the last card is drawn from the draw pile. You do not get to play that card. As soon as the draw pile is empty, stop and score. Both players may still have cards in their hands, and that is normal. Unplayed cards are simply ignored during scoring.

walkthrough.title: Scoring an Expedition
walkthrough.text: First, add up all the numbered cards in that column. Second, subtract twenty for the expedition cost. Third, multiply the result by one plus the number of investment cards. So one investment card means multiply by two, two means multiply by three, three means multiply by four. Finally, if the expedition has eight or more cards, add the twenty-point bonus.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Example
walkthrough.text: Here is an example. Let's say your blue expedition has two investment cards plus the three, five, seven, eight, and ten. The numbered cards total thirty-three. Subtract twenty to get thirteen. You have two investment cards, so multiply thirteen by three, which gives you thirty-nine points. That is a great expedition. Now imagine the same column with only the three and five. That totals eight, minus twenty equals negative twelve, times three equals negative thirty-six. Investment cards amplify losses too.

walkthrough.title: Unstarted Expeditions and Winning
walkthrough.text: Any expedition you did not start scores zero points, not negative twenty. You are never penalized for skipping a color entirely. After scoring all five expedition columns, add everything together for your round total. Play three rounds total and add up all three round scores. The player with the highest combined score across all three rounds wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with two players, Alice and Bob. We will play several turns so you can see how the game flows and learn from common mistakes along the way. Ready? Let's deal out eight cards to each player and begin.

walkthrough.title: Look at Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Let's say Alice looks at her eight cards and sees: a yellow investment card, yellow three, yellow seven, blue five, blue eight, green four, red six, and white nine. That is a lot of colors. Alice needs to decide which expeditions to pursue and which cards she will eventually discard.

walkthrough.title: Alice's First Turn: Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Alice decides to start yellow since she has three yellow cards including an investment card. She plays the yellow investment card first. Remember, investment cards must come before any numbered cards. Then she draws from the draw pile to refill her hand to eight. Good start.

walkthrough.title: Bob's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Bob has a red investment card, red four, red seven, red nine, green six, green eight, yellow ten, and blue three. Bob plays his red investment card to start a red expedition. He draws from the draw pile. Notice both players led with investment cards since those must be played before any numbers.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Mistake: Starting Too Many Expeditions
walkthrough.text: Alice gets excited and starts playing cards everywhere. She plays her blue five, then next turn her green four, then her red six. She now has four expeditions running at once. That is four times negative twenty, which means she needs eighty points in card values just to break even across all of them. With the deck running out, she will struggle to fill them all.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Start Too Many Expeditions
walkthrough.text: The most common beginner mistake is starting too many expeditions. Each one costs twenty points, so three expeditions means you are sixty points in the hole before playing a single number card. Focus on two or three strong expeditions rather than spreading yourself thin across all five colors. It is perfectly fine to leave some colors untouched.

walkthrough.title: Bob Plays Smart
walkthrough.text: Bob focuses on just two colors: red and green. He plays his red four onto his red investment card, keeping the ascending order. On his next turn he plays green six. Two focused expeditions with room to grow. Bob draws from the draw pile each time, keeping his options open.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Mistake: Discarding Carelessly
walkthrough.text: Alice has a green eight that she does not think she needs anymore. She discards it to the green discard pile without thinking about what Bob might want. Bob has been building a green expedition and happily picks up the green eight on his next turn. Alice just handed her opponent a free card.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch What You Discard
walkthrough.text: Before discarding, always glance at your opponent's expeditions. If they have started a color, any card you discard in that color is a gift to them. When you need to discard, try to put cards in colors your opponent has not started. If they have started everything, discard low cards that are less useful to them.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Mistake: Playing an Investment Too Late
walkthrough.text: Bob draws a second red investment card from the draw pile. He already played red four on top of his first investment card. He cannot play this new investment card because numbered cards are already in the red column. Investment cards must come before all numbers. That second investment card is now stuck in Bob's hand, dead weight.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Investment Timing Is Critical
walkthrough.text: You can never go back and add investment cards once you have played a number card. If you are thinking about investing in a color, play the investment cards right away before any numbers. Waiting is risky because once you play even a two, all investment cards in that color become useless. Sometimes you need to invest early even when you are not sure it will pay off.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game: Watch the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: We are about halfway through the round now. The draw pile is getting thinner. Alice counts the remaining cards and realizes there are only about fifteen left. That means roughly fifteen more turns between both players. She needs to focus on scoring as many points as possible in her existing expeditions rather than starting new ones.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Mistake: Drawing from a Discard Pile Too Early
walkthrough.text: Alice sees a white seven sitting on the white discard pile. She grabs it even though she has not started a white expedition and has no white cards in hand besides her nine. Now she has two white cards and feels pressured to start white. But starting another expedition this late is dangerous. She would have been better off drawing from the deck for a chance at cards in her existing colors.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Chase Discard Pile Cards
walkthrough.text: Just because a card is sitting face up does not mean you should take it. Drawing from a discard pile locks you into that color and can tempt you into starting an expedition you cannot profit from. Draw from the draw pile when you need flexibility. Only pick up discard pile cards when they fit perfectly into an expedition you are already building.

walkthrough.title: Smart Play: Blocking Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Bob notices Alice has a strong yellow expedition going with an investment card and several numbers. Bob draws a yellow six from the deck. He cannot use it since he has no yellow expedition. Instead of discarding it to the yellow pile where Alice could grab it, Bob holds onto it. He will discard it later in a color Alice does not care about, or save it to discard right before the game ends when Alice cannot use it.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: The draw pile runs out and the round is over. Let's score. Bob's red expedition has one investment card plus four, seven, and nine, totaling twenty. Subtract twenty to get zero, multiply by two for the investment card, and Bob gets zero points for red. That is a break-even, which is much better than a loss. His green expedition has six and eight, totaling fourteen minus twenty equals negative six. Bob scores negative six total for the round.

walkthrough.title: Before You Explore
walkthrough.text:
Congratulations on completing your first practice round of Lost Cities! You have seen how the game flows and learned from four common mistakes: starting too many expeditions, discarding cards your opponent wants, missing the investment card window, and chasing discard pile cards you do not need.

First, check out General Tips for deeper strategy guidance.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick overview of scoring and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun exploring!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, starting too many expeditions and not being able to fill any of them profitably. Second, playing investment cards on expeditions where you do not have enough numbered cards to justify the multiplier. Third, discarding cards your opponent is collecting without checking their expeditions first. Fourth, ignoring the draw pile count and getting surprised when the round ends. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: The Break-Even Threshold
walkthrough.text: The most important number in Lost Cities is twenty. That is the cost of every expedition, and your numbered cards need to total at least twenty to break even. Cards two through ten total fifty-four per color, so there is plenty of room to profit if you play enough cards. A useful shortcut: if you have three high cards in a color like six, eight, and ten, that is twenty-four, which clears the break-even threshold. But two low cards like three and five only total eight, leaving you twelve points in the hole.

walkthrough.title: When to Use Investment Cards
walkthrough.text: Investment cards are the highest-risk, highest-reward element in the game. A single investment card doubles your expedition score, which is amazing when you are profitable but devastating when you are not. The general rule is: only invest if you expect your numbered cards to total at least thirty. At thirty minus twenty, you have ten points, doubled to twenty. Below thirty, the risk outweighs the reward. With two or three investment cards, you need even more numbered cards to justify the multiplier.

walkthrough.title: Managing Your Discards
walkthrough.text: Discarding is the most interactive part of Lost Cities. Every card you place on a discard pile is available to your opponent. Before discarding, always look at your opponent's expedition columns. If they have started green, do not discard green cards. When you must get rid of a card, discard it in a color your opponent has not started. If they have started every color, discard the lowest card possible since low cards are the least useful. Late in the game, consider holding dead cards in your hand rather than gifting them.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Strategy
walkthrough.text: You have two drawing options each turn: the blind draw pile or a face-up discard pile. The draw pile gives you a random card but never helps your opponent. Picking from a discard pile gives you a known card but signals your intentions. If you pick up a green card, your opponent now knows you are building green and will stop discarding green cards for you. Draw from the deck when you want flexibility, and only take from discard piles when the card is perfect for an expedition you are already committed to.

walkthrough.title: Counting the Draw Pile
walkthrough.text: The draw pile is the game's clock, and tracking it is a crucial skill. There are forty-four cards in the starting draw pile after dealing sixteen to both players. Each turn removes one card from the pile, meaning roughly forty-four turns total split between both players. When the pile gets low, roughly ten cards or fewer, shift your focus. Stop starting new expeditions, play your highest-value cards on existing columns, and do not waste turns drawing cards you cannot use.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your opponent plays, discards, and draws. If they keep discarding blue cards, they probably do not have a blue expedition and blue is a safe color for you to discard in. If they pick up a card from the yellow discard pile, they are building yellow, so stop feeding them yellow cards. You can also watch which colors they play to estimate what cards remain in the deck. If your opponent has played red three, five, and eight, there are fewer red cards left for you to find.

walkthrough.title: The Three-Round Game
walkthrough.text: Lost Cities is played over three rounds, and your total score across all three rounds determines the winner. This means a bad round is not the end of the world. If Round 1 goes poorly, you can make it up in Rounds 2 and 3. Conversely, do not get complacent after a strong opening round. The cards reset each round, so stay sharp. Use early rounds to learn your opponent's tendencies and adjust your strategy in later rounds.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't start too many expeditions — each costs 20 pts
Don't invest in weak expeditions — multiplies losses too
Check opponent's expeditions before discarding
Watch the draw pile — don't get surprised by round end

bullets: Need numbered cards totaling 20+ to break even
Quick check: 3 high cards (6+8+10 = 24) clears the bar
2 low cards (3+5 = 8) leaves you -12 in the hole

Below 30, risk outweighs reward
More wagers = need even higher card totals to justify

summary.title: Managing Your Discards

If all colors started, discard lowest-value cards
Late game: sometimes hold dead cards rather than gift them

Discard pile = known card but signals your intentions
Only take from discard piles for cards that fit existing expeditions
Drawing from deck keeps your plans hidden

~44 turns total split between both players
At ~10 cards left: stop new expeditions, play high cards
Draw pile is the game clock — track it

Watch discard pile draws → colors they're building

Bad round isn't fatal — you can recover

Learn opponent's tendencies early, adjust later

walkthrough.title: Optimal Expedition Count
walkthrough.text: The sweet spot for most rounds is two or three expeditions. Two expeditions mean less risk and more focus, but you may leave points on the table if the cards come in your favor. Three expeditions offer more scoring potential but require more cards to break even. Starting four or five expeditions almost never works because the deck simply does not give you enough cards to fill them all. A good rule of thumb: commit to two expeditions early and only start a third if you draw heavily into that color.

walkthrough.title: Investment Card Math
walkthrough.text: Without investments, you need twenty points in numbered cards to break even. With one investment card, you still need twenty to break even because the multiplier hits both the positive and negative portions equally. But the upside doubles. With two investment cards, break-even is still twenty, but the upside triples. The real threshold is: invest when you have at least four or five numbered cards in that color in hand, or when you have seen very few cards of that color in the discard piles.

walkthrough.title: Chasing the Eight-Card Bonus
walkthrough.text: With three investment cards plus five numbered cards totaling twenty-five, you get twenty-five minus twenty equals five, times four equals twenty, plus the twenty-point bonus for a total of forty points. Compare that to the same five numbered cards without investments: twenty-five minus twenty equals five points. The bonus makes it worth playing low cards that you might otherwise skip.

walkthrough.title: Discard Pile Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use the discard piles as a strategic tool, not just a dump. You can intentionally discard a low card in a color your opponent wants to lure them into drawing it instead of a deck card. This burns one of their turns on a low-value card. You can also hold cards in colors your opponent needs to deny them resources. The key insight is that every card in the game is either in your hand, your opponent's hand, the draw pile, the discard piles, or already played. Tracking these locations gives you an information edge.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Pacing
walkthrough.text: Tempo in Lost Cities means controlling the speed at which the deck runs out. If you are ahead on points, you want the round to end quickly. Play cards and draw from discard piles to avoid thinning the deck. If you are behind, you want more time. Draw from the draw pile to deplete it faster so you get more turns. Yes, that sounds backwards, but remember: drawing from the deck reduces the deck faster than drawing from discard piles, which gives you more total turns before someone else empties it.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Discarding
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is to not play. If you have no good cards to add to your expeditions, you can discard a card and draw, essentially cycling your hand. The key is choosing what to discard defensively. Discard into colors with tall discard piles since your opponent is less likely to dig for those. Discard high cards in colors your opponent has not started, because starting an expedition late with a high card is usually not profitable for them. Never discard a card your opponent can immediately use to extend a strong expedition.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Serious Lost Cities players track which cards have been played or discarded. Each color has twelve cards: three investments plus numbers two through ten. If you have seen eight of the twelve red cards between your hand, the board, and both expedition areas, there are only four red cards left in the deck or your opponent's hand. This tells you the probability of drawing more red cards and whether your red expedition can grow further. You do not need to memorize everything, just pay attention to the high cards and investment cards in your target colors.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final ten cards of the draw pile, your strategy should shift entirely. Stop starting new expeditions. Stop taking risky draws from the deck. Focus on playing the highest-value cards you have onto your existing expeditions and discarding anything else safely. If you are holding a card your opponent needs, consider whether holding it one more turn to deny them points is worth more than playing a card to your own expedition. The last few turns often decide the round.

summary.title: Optimal Expedition Count
bullets: Sweet spot: 2-3 expeditions per round
2 = low risk, focused; 3 = more upside, more cards needed
4-5 expeditions almost never works — not enough deck cards
Commit to 2 early, add 3rd only if cards heavily favor it

summary.title: Investment Card Math
bullets: Break-even is always 20 in numbered cards, regardless of wagers
Wagers multiply upside: (30-20) × 2 = 20 vs (30-20) × 1 = 10
Invest when holding 4-5+ numbered cards in that color
Also invest when few cards of that color visible in discards

summary.title: Chasing the Eight-Card Bonus
bullets: 8+ cards = +20 bonus (added after multiplier)
3 wagers + 5 numbers (total 25): (25-20) × 4 + 20 = 40 pts
Makes low numbered cards worth playing for the count
Especially powerful with investment cards

summary.title: Discard Pile Manipulation
bullets: Discard low cards in opponent's color to waste their turn
Hold cards opponent needs to deny resources
Track card locations: hand, opponent hand, deck, discards, played
Information advantage = better decisions

summary.title: Tempo and Pacing
bullets: Ahead? Draw from discard piles → preserves deck, ends round sooner
Behind? Draw from deck → depletes it, gives more turns
Control the clock to match your scoring position
Tempo awareness separates beginners from advanced players

summary.title: Defensive Discarding
bullets: Discard into colors with tall discard piles (less likely to be taken)
Discard high cards in colors opponent hasn't started
Never discard cards opponent can immediately use
Sometimes cycling your hand is the best move

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: 12 cards per color: 3 investments + numbers 2-10
Track high cards and investments in your target colors
If 8 of 12 seen → only 4 left in deck/opponent hand
Probability awareness guides invest/discard decisions

summary.title: Endgame Optimization
bullets: Final ~10 cards: no new expeditions, no risky draws
Play highest-value cards on existing expeditions
Hold opponent's key cards to deny points
Last few turns often decide the round

entries.definition: A card with a handshake symbol instead of a number. Three per color. Must be played before any numbered cards in that expedition. Multiplies the expedition's final score: 1 wager = x2, 2 wagers = x3, 3 wagers = x4. Multiplies both profits and losses.
Nine cards per color, numbered two through ten. These are played in ascending order to expedition columns. Their values are summed during scoring.
One of the five expedition colors. Represents a journey to a desert or sandy environment. Contains cards 2-10 and three investment cards.
One of the five expedition colors. Represents an ocean or underwater exploration. Contains cards 2-10 and three investment cards.
One of the five expedition colors. Represents a snowy mountain or arctic expedition. Contains cards 2-10 and three investment cards.
One of the five expedition colors. Represents a jungle or rainforest exploration. Contains cards 2-10 and three investment cards.
One of the five expedition colors. Represents a volcanic or fiery landscape expedition. Contains cards 2-10 and three investment cards.
Cards in each expedition must be played from low to high. You can skip numbers (e.g., 3 then 7 is legal) but can never play a card lower than or equal to the last card played in that column.

Each color has its own face-up discard pile on the board. When you discard, place the card on its matching color pile. Either player can draw the top card of any discard pile instead of drawing from the deck.
The face-down pile of remaining cards after dealing. Players draw from here or from a discard pile at the end of each turn. When this pile is empty, the round ends immediately.
You cannot discard a card and then draw from that same color's discard pile on the same turn. You may discard one color and draw from a different color's discard pile.
Every expedition you start begins at negative twenty points. Your numbered cards must total more than twenty to make the expedition profitable. Unstarted expeditions score zero, not negative twenty.
If an expedition contains eight or more cards (including investment cards), add a flat twenty-point bonus. This bonus is added after the investment multiplier is applied.
Per expedition: (sum of numbered cards - 20) x (1 + number of investment cards) + 20 bonus if 8+ cards. Unstarted expeditions score 0.

The point at which an expedition scores zero instead of negative. This is always 20 points in numbered cards, regardless of how many investment cards are played. Below 20, the expedition loses points.
One complete play-through of the deck. Players alternate turns until the draw pile is exhausted. A full game consists of three rounds, with scores accumulated across all rounds.
Holding cards in your hand that your opponent needs, preventing them from getting those cards. A defensive tactic that sacrifices hand flexibility to deny your opponent points.
Discarding a card and drawing a new one to improve your hand without playing to an expedition. Useful when you have no profitable plays but want to keep searching for better cards.

The rate at which the draw pile depletes. Drawing from the deck reduces it faster (ending the round sooner), while drawing from discard piles preserves it. Players can manipulate tempo to control when the round ends.
Tracking which cards have been played, discarded, or are visible to estimate what remains in the deck and your opponent's hand. Each color has 12 cards total (3 investments + 9 numbered).
